inline_review:
commit c58730f41c369515a1caf9ebbe51cfa54a9ee3c7
Author: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
Subject: selftests/mm: add folio_split() and filemap_get_entry() race test

This commit adds a C port of a Rust test to reproduce a race condition
between folio_split() and filemap_get_entry(). The test creates shmem PMD
huge pages, punches holes using MADV_REMOVE, and concurrently reads the
shmem with multiple threads.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/CAKNNEtw5_kZomhkugedKMPOG-sxs5Q5OLumWJdiWXv+C9Yct0w@mail.gmail.com/ [1]
Link: https://github.com/dfinity/thp-madv-remove-test [2]

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/folio_split_race_test.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/folio_split_race_test.c
> new file mode 100644
> index 000000000000..efe4b631edfc
> --- /dev/null
> +++ b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/folio_split_race_test.c

[ ... ]

> +static bool check_page(unsigned char *base, size_t page_idx)
> +{
> +	unsigned char *page_ptr = base + page_idx * page_size;
> +	uint64_t expected_idx = (uint64_t)page_idx;
> +	uint64_t got_idx;
> +
> +	memcpy(&got_idx, page_ptr, 8);

Does this test actually trigger filemap_get_entry() in the kernel?

The readers use direct memory reads via memcpy on pages that were already
populated by fill_page(). Because the readers explicitly skip the
hole-punched pages, they only access memory that is fully mapped in the
hardware page tables (the remaining PTEs after the PMD is split by
MADV_REMOVE).

This means no page faults occur and no syscalls are made, so the kernel is
never entered and filemap_get_entry() is never executed by the readers.

Should the test use memfd_create() and pread() instead of userspace memory
reads to force concurrent kernel page cache lookups?

[ ... ]

> +static size_t run_iteration(void)
> +{

[ ... ]

> +	for (i = 0; i < TOTAL_PAGES; i++) {
> +		if (i % PUNCH_INTERVAL != 0)
> +			continue;
> +		if (madvise(mmap_base + i * page_size,
> +			    PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR * page_size, MADV_REMOVE) != 0) {
> +			ksft_exit_fail_msg(
> +				"madvise(MADV_REMOVE) failed on page %zu: %d\n",
> +				i, errno);
> +		}
> +
> +		i += PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR;

Does this loop advance the index further than intended?

The inner block adds PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR to the index, and then the for loop
header increments it again, effectively advancing by PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR + 1
after punching a hole.

Should the inner advancement be i += PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR - 1; instead to
avoid skipping the page immediately following the punched hole?

> +	}
> +
> +	atomic_store_explicit(&ctl.stop, 1, memory_order_release);

[ ... ]

> +static void thp_cleanup_handler(int signum)
> +{
> +	thp_restore_settings();

Is it safe to call thp_restore_settings() from a signal handler?

thp_restore_settings() calls thp_write_settings(), which relies on
non-async-signal-safe libc functions like fopen, fprintf, and snprintf.

If the process receives a signal while it is executing another
non-async-signal-safe function (such as malloc or vprintf inside
ksft_print_msg), could this corrupt internal libc state or deadlock on
standard I/O locks?

> +	/*
> +	 * Restore default handler and re-raise the signal to exit.
> +	 * This is to ensure the test process exits with the correct
> +	 * status code corresponding to the signal.
> +	 */
> +	signal(signum, SIG_DFL);
> +	raise(signum);
> +}

[ ... ]

> +int main(void)
> +{

[ ... ]

> +	ksft_print_msg("folio split race test\n");
> +	ksft_print_msg("===================================================\n");
> +	ksft_print_msg("Shmem size:       %zu MiB\n", FILE_SIZE / 1024 / 1024);
> +	ksft_print_msg("Total pages:     %zu\n", TOTAL_PAGES);

Can this format string cause issues on 32-bit architectures?

FILE_SIZE and TOTAL_PAGES are derived from uint64_t variables, but they are
printed using the %zu format specifier.

On 32-bit systems, size_t is 32 bits, which might cause vprintf to consume
only 32 bits from the 64-bit argument. Could this misalign the variadic
argument list and print garbage values for subsequent format specifiers?

> +	ksft_print_msg("Child readers:   %d\n", NUM_READER_THREADS);
> +	ksft_print_msg("Punching every %dth to %dth page\n", PUNCH_INTERVAL,
> +		       PUNCH_INTERVAL + PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR);
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"The test fails to trigger `filemap_get_entry()`, rendering it completely ineffective at reproducing the race condition it was designed to test.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"The commit states the test is designed to trigger a race condition between `folio_split()` and `filemap_get_entry()`. However, the reader threads use direct memory reads (`memcpy`) on pages that were already populated by `fill_page()`. Because the readers explicitly skip the hole-punched pages, they only access memory that is fully mapped in the hardware page tables (the remaining PTEs after the PMD is split by `MADV_REMOVE`). This means no page faults occur and no syscalls are made, so the kernel is never entered and `filemap_get_entry()` is never executed by the readers. The AI-generated C port incorrectly replaced the original Rust test's `pread()` syscalls with userspace memory reads. The test must use `memfd_create()` and `pread()` to force concurrent kernel page cache lookups."},{"problem":"Non-async-signal-safe functions are called from within a signal handler, which can cause deadlocks.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"The `thp_cleanup_handler` is registered to handle `SIGINT` and `SIGTERM`. It invokes `thp_restore_settings()`, which in turn calls `thp_write_settings()`. `thp_write_settings()` relies on non-async-signal-safe libc functions like `fopen`, `fprintf`, and `snprintf`. If the process receives a signal while it is executing another non-async-signal-safe function (such as `malloc` or `vprintf` inside `ksft_print_msg`), it can corrupt internal libc state or deadlock on standard I/O locks, causing the test suite to hang indefinitely."},{"problem":"Undefined behavior due to format string mismatch for `uint64_t` values.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"Variables `page_size` and `pmd_pagesize` are declared as `uint64_t`, meaning derived macros like `FILE_SIZE` and `TOTAL_PAGES` also evaluate to `uint64_t`. The test passes these 64-bit values to `ksft_print_msg()` using the `%zu` format specifier. On 32-bit architectures, `size_t` is 32 bits, so `vprintf` will incorrectly consume only 32 bits from the 64-bit argument. This misaligns the variadic argument list and leads to garbage values being printed for subsequent format specifiers in the same call."},{"problem":"Logic error in the hole-punching loop causes an unintended page skip.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"In `run_iteration()`, the hole-punching loop advances the index using `i += PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR;`. Because the `for` loop header also increments `i` via `i++`, the index is effectively advanced by `PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR + 1` after punching a hole. While this does not break the test with the current constants (3 and 50), it is an off-by-one logic error that silently skips evaluating the page immediately following the punched hole for the next loop iteration. The inner advancement should be `i += PUNCH_SIZE_FACTOR - 1;`."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit c58730f41c369515a1caf9ebbe51cfa54a9ee3c7
